headline: All seven majors sit below their 200-day lines while longs get liquidated and the ETF desks keep buying anyway
paragraphs: Start with the regime, because it is unambiguous today: BTC, ETH, SOL, XRP, BNB, DOGE and ADA are all trading below their 200-day averages, and none carries a golden cross. That is not a split market where you pick a side, it is one regime across the board. The distances tell you the pecking order within the downtrend: BTC is the least damaged at -47.8% from its 12-month high, ETH is off -60.9%, and the smaller names are worse, with DOGE at -76.0% and ADA at a rough -82.4%. When the largest asset is the healthiest chart in the room, that is the definition of a risk-off structure, not a rotation story.

Momentum tells a quieter story than the trend does. RSI readings are mostly parked in the mid-40s to high-50s (BTC 54.0, ETH 57.6, XRP 49.4, ADA 50.9), which is neutral, resting territory rather than oversold panic, with DOGE the exception at 32.8, closer to washed out. More notable: MACD sits above its signal line for six of the seven assets (all but SOL), even as every single one of them closed red today, DOGE hardest at -4.68%. That is a real divergence worth naming: short-term momentum is quietly firming while price prints red and the longer trend stays negative. Momentum improving underneath a falling tape is not a signal to act on, but it is a fact to watch.

Positioning adds friction to that picture. Funding is small and mixed, BTC longs pay 4.4% annualized, SOL 3.8%, XRP 3.6%, while ETH and DOGE longs actually collect a touch (-1.2% and -1.4%). Cheap funding usually means a trade isn't crowded, but the long/short account ratios say otherwise: traders are leaning long everywhere, from BTC's 1.54:1 up to XRP's 2.67:1 and DOGE's lopsided 5.4:1. That combination, crowded long books sitting on cheap funding, met a red day and the liquidation tape shows exactly who paid for it. BTC longs lost $16.8M against just $642K in shorts over the past 23 hours, ETH longs lost $20.3M against $1.1M, DOGE longs lost $2.0M against effectively nothing on the short side. The crowd was leaning one way and the tape leaned the other.

Flows are where the tension sharpens. Whale wallets moved a net $77.5M in BTC off exchanges in the last 24 hours, which on its face reads as accumulation. But the weekly series behind that number tells a fading story: net outflows ran $1.74B and then $976M in the two prior weekly windows, before turning to net figures of -$30.8M and -$192M in the two most recent ones. The strong withdrawal trend has stalled and tipped slightly toward exchanges again. Meanwhile the ETF tape, the traditional-finance side of the ledger, stayed positive through the selloff: BTC funds took in $69.1M net on the latest day (cumulative $51.9B), ETH funds took in $72.7M (cumulative $11.26B). Stablecoin float, the dry powder sitting on the sidelines, actually shrank 1.76% over the past 30 days to $307.3B, so that reserve is a touch smaller than it was a month back, not building into this weakness.

Sentiment closes the loop as the foil it usually is. Fear & Greed reads 31, labeled Fear, but that sits at the top of its own 30-day range of 11 to 33, meaning the crowd is, if anything, the least afraid it has been all month even as every major asset sold off today and leveraged longs took the liquidation hit. The tape (soft price, longs punished, whale accumulation trend cooling) and the sentiment gauge (calmer than its recent past) are not telling the same story right now, and that gap is itself the day's finding.

What to watch from here: whether BTC's weekly whale net flips back to a clean, sustained outflow figure like the $1.74B and $976M readings from two and three weeks back, whether ETF net flows turn negative on a session where price is also falling (today they did not), any funding flip into meaningfully negative territory across the board rather than the mixed picture now, and, structurally, whether BTC's price manages to close back above its 200-day line, the one condition that would change the regime call at the top of this note rather than just the mood inside it.
